global.title: Swarm /bytes content-addressing means identical uploads dedupe automatically at the network layer
global.content:
Swarm /bytes chunks are content-addressed: the reference is derived from the chunk's content (BMT hash). Two consequences for duplication analysis:

**1. Same data -> same reference, regardless of uploader.** If Alice and Bob both upload the same encrypted operation batch, they produce identical chunk references. The network stores it once.

**2. Immutability is intrinsic.** Once written, a chunk cannot change — changing the content would change the reference. This is why /bytes is durable until stamp expiration and why "versioning" requires feeds (mutable pointers) layered on top.

**Implications for our duplication picture**:
- Our encrypted operation batches are per-user (encryption key differs), so cross-user de-dup is limited in practice.
- But within a user's own writes, re-uploading the same batch (e.g., after a retry) is effectively free — the network already has the chunk.
- This is different from PostgreSQL/IndexedDB where "duplicate" means "both rows exist and take up bytes twice."

**Terminology care**: when the analysis says "operations exist in both PGlite and Swarm," the Swarm side is the content-addressed chunk — a single chunk even if uploaded many times. PGlite, by contrast, stores raw rows with no content-addressing.
global.description: Because /bytes chunks are content-addressed, uploading the same data twice yields the same reference — the network deduplicates for free, independent of who uploaded it.
